items.question: Who uses decision trees in practice?
items.answer: Data scientists use them as interpretable tabular baselines, for rule extraction in regulated domains (credit, healthcare), and as the base learner inside random forests and gradient boosting pipelines.

items.question: What is a decision tree (CART)?
items.answer: A supervised model that recursively splits data with binary axis-aligned rules (feature j ≤ t), routing samples to leaves that predict majority class or mean target. CART grows trees greedily by maximizing impurity reduction.

items.question: When should you choose a decision tree over logistic regression?
items.answer: When you need nonlinear, local decision boundaries, automatic feature interactions, or human-readable rules — and you will constrain depth to control variance. Prefer logistic regression for linear boundaries, calibrated probabilities with less tuning, or very small high-noise data.

items.question: Where does impurity reduction happen in the algorithm?
items.answer: At each internal node: CART evaluates candidate splits on all features, computes weighted Gini or entropy (classification) or variance (regression) of child nodes, and keeps the split with largest gain.

items.question: Why do unconstrained trees overfit?
items.answer: Each extra split can isolate training noise into tiny leaves with pure or near-pure class counts. Without depth or leaf-size limits, the tree memorizes the training set — low bias on train, high variance on new data.

items.question: How do you predict for a new sample?
items.answer: Start at the root, evaluate each node's split test on x, follow left or right child, repeat until a leaf, return the leaf's stored class (majority vote) or numeric value (mean y).

items.question: Gini impurity vs entropy — when do they differ?
items.answer: Both measure class mixing; both are zero for pure nodes. Entropy uses log terms and is measured in bits; Gini is algebraic and slightly faster. They usually pick the same best split; treat criterion as secondary to depth and leaf constraints.

items.question: Decision tree classifier vs regressor — same algorithm?
items.answer: Same CART recursive structure. Classifier: Gini/entropy impurity, majority class at leaves. Regressor: variance/MSE impurity, mean target at leaves. Problem type changes impurity and prediction, not the greedy split search.

items.question: Single decision tree vs random forest — trade-off?
items.answer: Single tree: fully interpretable path, fast inference, high variance. Random forest: averages many trees — higher accuracy and stability, lower interpretability per tree. Forests assume you understand CART splits first.

items.question: Decision tree vs k-nearest neighbors — complexity control?
items.answer: Trees control capacity via depth and leaf size (discrete rules). kNN controls smoothness via k (local voting). Trees produce explicit rules; kNN stores all training points and has no training phase beyond indexing.

items.question: What if max_depth is set to 1 (a decision stump)?
items.answer: Only one split from root to two leaves — high bias, low variance. Useful as a weak learner in boosting or as the simplest interpretable baseline, but it cannot capture multi-step interactions.

items.question: What if a leaf is pure (all one class) but min_samples_leaf is not met at the parent?
items.answer: CART may still split if min_samples_split allows it and impurity gain exceeds min_impurity_decrease. Pure leaves stop further splitting on that branch — entropy and Gini are already zero.

items.question: What if you forget to encode a nominal city column as integers 0..N without one-hot?
items.answer: The tree treats city codes as ordered numbers — splits like city ≤ 2 impose a false ordering across unrelated city names, producing meaningless rules and poor generalization.

items.question: Cause: Increase max_depth on a 300-sample dataset. Effect?
items.answer: Training error falls; leaves become smaller and more specific; validation error often drops then rises as variance dominates — classic U-shaped complexity curve.

items.question: Cause: Switch from Gini to entropy with max_depth=5 fixed. Effect?
items.answer: Tree structure may change slightly at tie-break splits, but validation metrics usually shift little. Depth and leaf constraints matter far more than criterion choice.

items.question: Cause: Add one-hot columns for a 50-level category. Effect?
items.answer: Tree can split on each level independently (column ≤ 0.5), avoiding false ordinal structure — but feature space grows and split search cost increases.
